_comment: Per-chart narratives. CI (build_prompt.py -> GitHub Models -> write_narrative.py) overwrites these from live numbers; this committed copy is the fallback so the page always reads well. source = 'fallback' | 'github-models'.

notes.shape: The yield curve plots Treasury yields across maturities from one month to 30 years, where a normally upward-sloping curve indicates higher yields for longer maturities, reflecting expectations of economic growth and inflation. An inverted curve, where shorter-term yields exceed longer-term yields, has historically preceded US recessions by 6 to 18 months. Currently, the 10-year Treasury yield stands at 4.57%, and the 2s10s spread—the difference between 10-year and 2-year yields—is 0.37 percentage points, indicating a positively sloped curve. This contrasts with the deep inversion between 2022 and 2024, when the spread was at or below 0.0 points, signaling a previously inverted curve now re-steepened above zero.
notes.signal: The 2s10s spread is a well-established recession indicator; its inversion has historically predicted recessions. The deepest inversion recorded was a spread of 0.0 points, with the curve inverted through much of 2022 to 2024. The recent positive spread of 0.37 points shows the curve has un-inverted, which, in prior cycles, often occurs shortly before a recession begins, thus complicating simple interpretations. Concurrently, the New York Fed recession probability remains elevated at 31%, suggesting a material risk of recession despite the now positively sloped curve.
notes.now: The current positively sloped yield curve, with a 2s10s spread of 0.37 points and a 10-year yield at 4.57%, indicates a normalization from the prior inversion. Combined with a 31% NY Fed recession probability, the signal is mixed: while the curve is no longer inverted, the elevated recession risk remains notable. This suggests caution but does not specify timing or certainty of an economic downturn.
